> @dmitriz 
> You don't even need to think about how useful this or that metric is. Your readers are grown up enough to figure this out for themselves. Give it to them, and they will use it the way they want and they will thank you for the choice. ;-)

I don't think ANY metric can be used universally to solve the problem of lists gathering dust. Different communities just have different dynamics. And even within a community, often you need multiple metrics to find what you are looking for.
I myself, work in academia, where also tons of metrics are used to gauge the performance of researchers, journals, institutes, etc. The values can differ by orders of magnitude across fields and learning what is a good or a poor value, is something you only learn by being in the field.
Also, it gives a very one-dimensional view of "popularity", but often you rather want to see "theoretical" vs "practical", "easy" vs "difficult", things that are hard or impossible to capture in a metric.

Just to say: I think there is little merit in picking a metric at the top level and suggesting this to other repositories. You readers may eventually learn in their own field what a "good" or a "poor" value for a metric is, but that also takes quite a bit of experience: something you only learn by reading the actual items. So metrics do not help the novices to find good content, but rather help more seasoned users.

> @dmitriz 
> It is hard for me to imagine how that would work at scale in practice. Manually going through hundreds of links requires a lot of time. It is honourable when people find the time to do it, to benefit community, however, it is not what the community should automatically expect or feel entitled to. And even if maintainers want to commit, it can put a lot of pressure on them and get them burned out and dropping everything else, if the overload gets too high. You don't even need to search hard for an example.

You say that maintainers already have a lot to do to keep track of their items.  I would even say that keeping metrics is not going to make the situation of a maintainer any better, but rather worse. Once you introduce metrics, you also need to keep the metrics updated.

Personally, I think the current approach of manual checking is fine. Moreover, if there is an involved community, this is something that the users will notice. E.g. if a link goes stale, I would expect the readers of that list to voice their concerns. The detection of basic problems (broken links) can be automated, but I think that is where it stops as well.

> @dmitriz 
> That is certainly useful idea but it raises few questions:
> - How many maintainers are actually using it and how does the reader see this? Is there any kind of badge for it?
> - Once the link is found broken, what are the actions?
> - Are the links automatically removed from the list?
> - Or perhaps project is moved elsewhere and simple search can uncover the new link? Is this process automated?
> - What if the link is temporarily down for whatever reason?
> - What if the link is intact but the content is still not so awesome any more?

Some people use a CI server to run that script, this can be seen e.g. from a `.travis.yml` file or something similar.
While your questions regard `awesome_bot` (which only checks dead links and redirects), similar issues also exist for updating timestamps or other metrics:
- How big a change is needed to update the timestamp? 10 characters? 10% of the page?
- Where would you store the cached version of the page (to check whether the new version has changed)? How about copyright/IP restrictions?
- How to deal with pages that are redesigned (same content, different stylesheet)?
- How to deal with sites that are replaced (e.g. hijacked, removed and eventually replaced by spam sites, ...)? Those will differ a lot from the previous version.
- What if the link is temporarily down for whatever reason?
- What if the new content is not awesome?
- How often would you check for updates? Every day? Every week? Won't that clutter the history of the project with lots of automatic updates?

It cannot be avoided that there is manual work involved. I just have the feeling that keeping track of more things (even assisted with an automatic procedure), will just cause a lot more work for limited benefit.

> > @dmitriz
> > You don't even need to think about how useful this or that metric is. Your readers are grown up enough to figure this out for themselves. Give it to them, and they will use it the way they want and they will thank you for the choice. ;-)
>

> :+1:

But let's not forget that these are "curated lists of awesome", we shouldn't leave robots to completely run and ensure the validity for links in projects :smile:

I agree with this: let bots/scripts help with the work, but it remains essentially manual work since there is no bot that can check "is X awesome?". Awesomeness will always remain a subjective matter.

The current `/update-my-email` flow updates `newEmail`, `emailVerified`, and `emailVerifyTTL` before checking `emailAuthLinkTTL` for the general resend rate limit. So a second request to a different address within the five-minute window returns `429`, but still changes `user.newEmail`. That then causes the original `/confirm-email` link to fail because it no longer matches the stored pending email.

A fix should move the `emailAuthLinkTTL` rate-limit check ahead of the user update, so a rate-limited request exits without mutating the pending email state. Please include a regression test for the "request A, immediately request B, B returns 429 and `newEmail` remains A" case.

The bug lives in the `Ctrl+Enter` handler in both `hotkeys.tsx` and the Monaco keybindings in `editor.tsx`.
Currently, the logic relies on `!isProjectBased(challengeType)` to decide whether to submit or re-run tests. However, `jsLab` and `pyLab` are included in `projectBasedChallengeTypes`. Because `isProjectBased()` returns `true` for labs, the code always falls through to the `else` branch, which unconditionally calls `executeChallenge()` instead of `submitChallenge()`.

The "Submit & Continue" button works correctly because it uses a different rendering branch (`IndependentLowerJaw`) that doesn't rely on this specific hotkey logic.

I implemented the following locally:

1. Added an `isLabChallenge()` utility and updated the condition to `!isProjectBased(challengeType) || isLabChallenge(challengeType)`.
2. If a user passes the tests but then modifies their code, `Ctrl+Enter` should re-run the tests, not immediately navigate away. I added a `useRef` (`submittedCodeRef`) to snapshot the code contents upon passing.

https://github.com/wvwwvwwv/scalable-con:
Hi,


I guess a sync-blocking method was used in `fn poll` in your case; SCC allows sync/async methods to be used in parallel, and won't lead to a deadlock if not used in `fn poll` or keeping an `Entry` across await points. Type separation will definitely prevent this from happening, but I'm afraid to say that this does not solve all the deadlock issues as the `Entry` API and many others still allow a thread to acquire multiple locks on different entries.
-> The problem surface that your suggestion addresses seems to be too narrow.

What I would do instead is to clearly note any possible deadlock scenarios in the doc, including examples, and keep the API as it is for now.
-> It is clear that the use of sync methods in `fn poll` is problematic. This should have been clearer.


That said, if an API change can make the crate more deadlock resilient, I'll readily make a huge change in version 3.

Maybe something like the following?

```
// map does not provide any methods to insert/update/delete entries.
let map = scc::HashMap::default();

let mut map_handle = map.write_sync(); // fn write_sync(&self) -> MapWriter<'_, ..>.
map_handle.insert(1, 1); // fn insert(&mut self, K, V) -> Result<..>.

let mut async_handle = map.write_async(); // fn write_async -> MapAsyncWriter<'_, ...>.
//
```

> I guess AtomicShared<T> suffices

Yes, but I think simpler APIs could be provided such as: 

```rust
pub fn get(&self, impl FnMut(Option<&T>));
pub fn get_mut(&self, impl FnMut(Option<&mut T>));
pub fn set(&self, val: T);
pub fn take(&self) -> T;
pub fn del(&self); // other names: delete, remove
```
instead of the complex things:
```rust
pub fn load<'g>(&self, order: Ordering, _guard: &' g Guard) -> Ptr<'g, T>;
pub fn swap(&self, new: (Option<Shared<T>>, tag: Tag), order: Ordering) -> (Option<Shared<T>>, Tag);
```

> Can you elaborate on it, e.g., interfaces? Allowing a mutable reference means exclusive access to the inner value, meaning concurrent read access will be unsafe.

This is my suggestion, but it may be lacking:

Maintains two inner values (may pointers or mutable cells?): one for the `old value`, and one for the `new value`.
* This requires that `T` implements the `Clone` trait.
* Whether the current value is an `old value` or a `new value` is indicated by an AtomicBool, where `false` means `old value` and `true` means `new value`. so of these two values, the `old value` and the `new value` are interchangeable.
* `new()` or some other method creates the container: AtomicBool is set to `false` (it defaults to `old value`), then sets `old value` to `Some(T.clone())` and sets `new value` to `Some(T)`.
* `get_mut()` can only get the `new value` and flips the internal AtomicBool (`AtomicBool::fetch_not(Ordering)`) that when it finishes
* whereas `get()` can only get the `old value` until `get_mut()` finishes (that is, when the `new value` has been mutable accessed, it can only get immutable the `old value`).
* if `get_mut()` changed `new value` during `get()`, overwrite `old value` with `new value` after the last immutable reference dropped.

This has the following problems or features:

* it is not updated in real-time because it maintains two values, and in fact should not be updated in "real-time" when there are immutable references.
* This requires inner value to be capable of cloning, since two values in different memory locations must be stored at the same time.
* <del> This avoids the unnecessary performance overhead of "first get and clone, then modify it, finally overwrite back" every time (if a change is needed) </del> But this problem is not completely solved, as it seems to require writing back to the "old value" after updating the "new value" (if all immutable references are dropped)

https://github.com/ds300/jetzt/issues/45:
Ahh we've I didn't have a regex rule for that. I've updated for that

The current pattern is 

``` js
/['’](?=(([dst]|nt|es|re|ve)[\s$\n])|cause|em|cept|tis|\d{2})/gi
/s['’](?=\s|\n)/gi
```

Which basically means any quotes that are followed by a d,s,t, "nt", "es", "re", "ve" then a space or followed by cause,em, cept or tis or two digits are not a part of a quotation.

I think I might give up on the single quote parsing though, it's a nightmare and will not work in all situations without some "moon-shot" investment of time!

I really appreciate you taking a look though, I hope I have explored some concepts. I feel drained now and will just wait to see what @ds300 comes up with next and reconcile with that.

"Is something with your pivots different from the original?"

Yes when I call the get pivot function, I strip out any punctuation or anything that isn't a letter basically, so ".", "-", ";" etc will come out. This may slightly shift the balance to the left!
https://github.com/ds300/jetzt/issues/24:
Hi @peteruithoven :)

Well I might be going out on a limb here, but I really like the concept of seeing a real time highlight update in the underlying web page. I think the context experience is one that's better in real-time. The counter arguments is that it's computationally slow, visually messy i.e. damaging the style of the underlying web site or just visually distracting. 

I think the real time idea adds value. Opinions?

My current vision for the context is something like this (similar to the image posted by @danjul)

![image](https://f.cloud.github.com/assets/6911390/2404737/99057e62-aa41-11e3-8821-05eb124ca57f.png)

On my first hash of this (see https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jebPFbVdmTw) I turned all words into SPANS. This upset the style of the underlying web page. 

So the new idea is that the we float a div over the paragraph currently being read. I think also that the jetzt reader should be "visually coupled" to this paragraph in some way but in such a way that the jetzt control maintains a fixed viewport position. When we move to the next paragraph we can do a fancy non linear translate/scale transform on the paragraph overlay. We inject into this div all the words and do the real time highlighting. This confers several benefits. 1) it's computationally simple 2) it doesn't manipulate the underlying web page at all 3) it gives beautiful context

Discuss :)
https://github.com/jenkinsci/docker-plug:
> hey @MarkEWaite.
> 
> I saw that apache-httpcomponents-client-5-api-plugin has a [new release](https://github.com/jenkinsci/apache-httpcomponents-client-5-api-plugin/releases/tag/5.4-124.v31e2987e48f4). Any plans to release a new docker-plugin version (maybe 1.7.1) that depends on it? 

A new release of the docker plugin is not needed.  Users can install that new release of the httpclient 5 plugin and it will be used by the docker plugin.  

Even though no release is needed, I did update the docker plugin to rely on the most recent httpclient 5 plugin release in this pull request:

* https://github.com/jenkinsci/docker-plugin/pull/1114

> Do you think it could solve [#1103](https://github.com/jenkinsci/docker-plugin/issues/1103) ?

Yes, I've confirmed that it fixes the issue.  That release fixes the issue by reverting the library inside the httpclient 5 plugin to 5.3.1.  The [apache-httpcomponents-client-5-api-plugin 5.4-124.v31e2987e48f4 changelog](https://github.com/jenkinsci/apache-httpcomponents-client-5-api-plugin/releases/tag/5.4-124.v31e2987e48f4) says:

> * Use httpclient 5.3.1, not httpclient 5.4 (https://github.com/jenkinsci/apache-httpcomponents-client-5-api-plugin/pull/62) @MarkEWaite

I've left issue #1103 open because the [permanent fix](https://github.com/docker-java/docker-java/pull/2364) has been submitted to the docker-java library and needs to be merged and released by one of the maintainers of the docker-java library.

https://github.com/Poly-Haven/polyhavena: > Hi there! > > Blender seems to have changed its default import behaviour again, bringing in asset collections as non-instances rather than the old default of making them instances of the collection, which allows us to change the reference the instance is pointing to and therefore switching the LOD. > > I'll need to investigate a proper solution to this, but for now the workaround is simple: After dragging in an asset, check the 'Instance' checkbox in the redo pano. Then you should see the LOD selector again. > > > I'll keep this issue open and update the title to remind me to to figure out a better way to deal with LODs now that Blender defaults to non-instances. I guess this a better place to keep the comments from the Fedora update linked above as they are more applicable to the PKI project :) Tomcat 10.1 is current incompatible with this application. I dug into the automated testing which are failing and found a few things to get you started on updates.

1. The catalina.properties file needs to add a few more jars to the exclusion list so that scanning them won't fail. Failures look like:

```
May 31 10:20:20 ipa002.test.openqa.fedoraproject.org server[4859]: WARNING: Failed to scan [file:/usr/share/java/tomcat-jakartaee-migration/bcel-6.8.1.jar] from classloader hierarchy
May 31 10:20:20 ipa002.test.openqa.fedoraproject.org server[4859]: java.nio.file.NoSuchFileException: /usr/share/java/tomcat-jakartaee-migration/bcel-6.8.1.jar
May 31 10:20:20 ipa002.test.openqa.fedoraproject.org server[4859]:         at java.base/sun.nio.fs.UnixException.translateToIOException(UnixException.java:92)
....
```

and can be fixed with a patch such as:

```
--- conf/catalina.properties.orig   2024-05-31 19:45:43.846548383 -0400
+++ conf/catalina.properties    2024-05-31 19:46:22.194548383 -0400
@@ -139,7 +139,7 @@ junit.jar,junit-*.jar,hamcrest-*.jar,eas
 objenesis-*.jar,ant-launcher.jar,\
 cobertura-*.jar,asm-*.jar,dom4j-*.jar,icu4j-*.jar,jaxen-*.jar,jdom-*.jar,\
 jetty-*.jar,oro-*.jar,servlet-api-*.jar,tagsoup-*.jar,xmlParserAPIs-*.jar,\
-xom-*.jar
+xom-*.jar,bcel*.jar,commons-compress*.jar,jakartaee-migration-*.jar

 \# Default list of JAR files that should be scanned that overrides the default
 \# jarsToSkip list above. This is typically used to include a specific JAR that
```

Note: this is not a complete list and will probably need others.

2. Tomcat 10.1 supports jakartaee instead of javaee, so there's failures such as:

```
May 31 10:20:22 ipa002.test.openqa.fedoraproject.org server[4859]: SEVERE: Begin event threw exception
May 31 10:20:22 ipa002.test.openqa.fedoraproject.org server[4859]: java.lang.NoClassDefFoundError: javax/servlet/http/HttpServletResponse
May 31 10:20:22 ipa002.test.openqa.fedoraproject.org server[4859]:         at java.base/java.lang.Class.getDeclaredConstructors0(Native Method)
....
```

3. The last item that I noticed are issues with tomcatjss and the API changes in tomcat 10:

```
May 31 10:20:23 ipa002.test.openqa.fedoraproject.org server[4859]: SEVERE: Error reading request, ignored
May 31 10:20:23 ipa002.test.openqa.fedoraproject.org server[4859]: java.lang.AbstractMethodError: Receiver class org.dogtagpki.jss.tomcat.JSSImplementation does not define or inherit an implementation of the resolved method 'abstract org.apache.tomcat.util.net.SSLSupport getSSLSupport(javax.net.ssl.SSLSession, java.util.Map)' of abstract class org.apache.tomcat.util.net.SSLImplementation.
May 31 10:20:23 ipa002.test.openqa.fedoraproject.org server[4859]:         at org.dogtagpki.jss.tomcat.JSSSecureNioChannel.getSSLSupport(JSSSecureNioChannel.java:327)
....
May 31 10:20:23 ipa002.test.openqa.fedoraproject.org server[4859]:         at java.base/java.lang.Thread.run(Thread.java:1583)
May 31 10:20:23 ipa002.test.openqa.fedoraproject.org server[4859]: SEVERE: Error running socket processor
May 31 10:20:23 ipa002.test.openqa.fedoraproject.org server[4859]: java.lang.NullPointerException: Cannot invoke "java.nio.ByteBuffer.limit(int)" because "this.byteBuffer" is null
May 31 10:20:23 ipa002.test.openqa.fedoraproject.org server[4859]:         at org.apache.coyote.http11.Http11InputBuffer.recycle(Http11InputBuffer.java:262)
....
```

> Are you sure about calling it ci.github_actions instead of just ci.github? Later looks better, even if it's not as precise

Fair enough. I'll update the issue description to match that

> It's not clear to me the separation between "low" and "high" level interfaces. I think "low level" would be writing workflows and "high level" would be turning knobs and picking workflows for the library.

My idea was that “low level” is directly writing workflows using GitHub's schema (except in Nickel), while “high-level” is providing an opinionated library of relevant workflows for projects using Organist. Does that match your understanding?

> add a flag to enable GitHub workflow generation, like ci.github.enable = true, so that we can template the whole project at once

Yes, we'll need to keep that in mind indeed. Although reality will probably remind us of it if we ever forget 😉

> About the example: it contains flake.lock file that must point to old version of nickel-nix itself. Please try to update nickel-nix input in it.

That worked! For googlers, i ran `nix flake lock --update-input nickel-nix` and `nix run .#regenerate-lockfile`.

> About features: I don't think nickel-nix is ready for full blown packaging in Nickel, unless you're ready to reimplement all nixpkgs frameworks in it. We pass Nix objects into Nickel world and back, and we're very limited in what we can pass. For example, we cannot pass closures (functions, callbacks) that are wildly used in nixpkgs.

Yea this (with examples) is exactly what is missing from documentation, imo. Though with this i'm still a bit unclear on what NickelNix _can_ do. Shame that Nix can't run from some intermediate static config format (JSON/etc) that Nickel could then just compile to.

For context i'd be looking to replace my Nix system configuration with Nickel. I'm very unclear on where the interop occurs, but at the very least i imagine Home Manager would pose issues. I am not currently trying to distribute Nickel as a package for Nix users. Not that it makes much of a distinction when compared to system configuration, probably.

What are some use cases for Nickel Nix as it is usable today? I see devShells and i have them working locally, though i'm unsure how big the gulf is between devshell flakes and system flakes (nixosConfiguration/home manager/etc). Thoughts?

Hi @pablogs9, I have some updated captures that show the 2 types of delay more clearly.  The trace can be opened with [Saleae Logic](https://www.saleae.com/downloads/).  The zip also includes a `-v6` agent log file.  The baud rate used is 115200.

[adi_micro-ros_tx_image_captures.zip](https://github.com/micro-ROS/micro_ros_setup/files/13482177/adi_micro-ros_tx_image_captures.zip)

### Delays between each Transport Unit

Here is an image that highlights the delays between each image data packet (I hope "Transport Unit" is the right term here?).  

On average it's between 200-300ms per TU.

![image](https://github.com/micro-ROS/micro_ros_setup/assets/38844790/494f773c-3fe3-4ae2-b8e6-2d71870aecdf)

When the red "Indicator" line is high, the code is inside my custom serial write function.  Here is a closer look between two TUs.

![image](https://github.com/micro-ROS/micro_ros_setup/assets/38844790/dd8bb5c8-e849-47cb-92e4-3c1a0864467d)

### Delays inside each Transport Unit

This image shows the delay between the frame and data portions of the transport unit.  It's actually worse than the 1ms delay I captured on the RX side, since it looks like the publisher is waiting on a response from the agent for the frame.

The delay originally varied between 1-16ms.

![image](https://github.com/micro-ROS/micro_ros_setup/assets/38844790/3e10f704-1e71-4c61-9d34-09b930baf899)
 
After I decreased my USB latency timer with `echo 1 > /sys/bus/usb-serial/devices/ttyUSB1/latency_timer`, the variability improved to about 2-3ms.

![image](https://github.com/micro-ROS/micro_ros_setup/assets/38844790/e72a2c78-fa39-47d7-9c32-68488dd6cca8)

### Continuous Serialization?

So - basically I would like to know if continuous serialization would let me bypass most of the frame/packeting requirements for the image data.  Ideally I'd like to just send one frame, and then manually serialize the data as I receive it.

Thanks for your support,
Jake

On the Raspberry pi I ran those commands in my ros2 workspace after having sourced the main ros2 installation (humble).
```
git clone -b $ROS_DISTRO https://github.com/micro-ROS/micro_ros_setup.git
rosdep update && rosdep install --from-paths src --ignore-src -y
colcon build
source install/local_setup.bash 
ros2 run micro_ros_setup create_agent_ws.sh 
ros2 run micro_ros_setup build_agent.sh
source install/local_setup.bash 
ros2 run micro_ros_agent micro_ros_agent serial --dev /dev/ttyAMA1 -b 921600 -v 6
```

on the px4 side I have executed the f[ollowing command](https://docs.px4.io/main/en/modules/modules_system.html#microdds-client) in the NuttX-shell: 
```
microdds_client start -t serial -d /dev/ttyS2 -b 921600
```
And the two devices are connected through a uart connection using 3 cables: GND, TX and RX.

The serial connection works, I can access the data through `screen`.

If you need more detailed information or logfiles (do they exist?) I'm happy to share those as well.

I also think there's something wrong. Strictly, the requested_data cannot be larger than prefetch_buf_size. Also, `self.next_col_prefetch_idx` should be updated in the func. Besides, the code in the following lines

```
        if requested_data_size > self.active_buf_size:
            self.next_line_prefetch_idx = num_lines % self.fetch_matrix.shape[0]
        else:
            self.next_line_prefetch_idx = (num_lines + 1) % self.fetch_matrix.shape[1]
```
seems to be wrong. I think it should be  

```
        if requested_data_size > self.active_buf_size:
            self.next_line_prefetch_idx = (self.next_line_prefetch_idx + num_lines) % self.fetch_matrix.shape[0]
        else:
            self.next_line_prefetch_idx = (self.next_line_prefetch_idx + num_lines + 1) % self.fetch_matrix.shape[0]
```

But they don't affect the results. I think we don't need to care about the "fake data" from DRAM. As the stall cycle is decided by hashed_buf (whether the addr in buf) and num_lines (the size of prefetched buf).

Hi @datMaffin , 
Apologies for late response. I finally got some time to come back and update the tool. 

Let me try to explain your observations. 

**1. Pauses in execution which are not seen in v1**
This is very good question. The reason for the pauses is that in v2 the trace format is modified to reflect the pipeline fill and drain times in the folds. The trace which we observe in v1, requires some extra hardware supports to completely drain the array using extra links other than the peer to peer links. In the new version I wanted to model the behavior of the simplest systolic array so that users have a clean baseline. 
BTW SCALE-Sim-v2 can be modified to model the enhancements in the systolic array as well, but that is not the goal of the base simulator. 

Having said that I still observe some explained pauses, (addition of a cycle or two) which I am assuming are some indexing errors and I will take a look. 

**2. The cycles for SRAM read and write in the output do not line up.**
Thanks I need to check this.

**3. Weird errors with DRAM BW sweep**
It would be great if you can provide me with your config and topology files, I will look into it. 

Thanks for your contributions. 

Cheers

https://github.com/Frodo45127/rpfm/issue:
# Update — root cause is in rpfm_server's load path, not the generator

Hey, I debugged this with AI assistance (Claude Code). I can't repro the "generator skips .pack contents" angle from the issue body. Byte-grepping the on-disk `wh3.pak{1,2,3}` finds ~10k `db/` paths and ~428k `animations/` strings in the cache, and my patched build's load reports `vanilla_files=685348, vanilla_tables=1521` cleanly. The cache is fine. The bug is downstream.

Patched HEAD locally to surface the swallowed errors and trace `Dependencies::rebuild`. Found two clear server-side bugs:

## Bug 1: `background_thread.rs:572` silently eats rebuild errors

```rust
let _ = dependencies.write().unwrap().rebuild(&None, ...);
```

`Err` from `rebuild()` is discarded, no log. Combined with `*self = Self::default()` running before the load attempt in `rebuild()`, any failure leaves the dep state empty with zero diagnostics. Made this very painful to chase from the outside.

Fix:

```rust
if let Err(e) = dependencies.write().unwrap().rebuild(&None, ...) {
    error!("Dependencies rebuild failed in SetGameSelected: {e:#}");
}
```

## Bug 2: `background_thread.rs:571` ignores `rebuild_dependencies=true` after the first call

```rust
let file_path = if game_changed { Some(&*file_path) } else { None };
```

First `set_game_selected(warhammer_3, true)` sets `first_game_change_done = true`. Second call with same game gives `game_changed = false`, so `file_path = None` and `rebuild()` skips the cache-load block. Caller's explicit `rebuild_dependencies=true` silently lost.

From my patched logs:

```
[first call, works]
DLW-DIAG: rebuild() entering file_path Some branch, path="…\wh3.pak2"
DLW-DIAG: rebuild() loaded cache, vanilla_files=685348, vanilla_tables=1521
DLW-DIAG: rebuild() needs_updating=false
DLW-DIAG: rebuild() assigned stored_data to self

[second call, same game, silent no-op]
DLW-DIAG: rebuild() file_path is None, skipping cache load entirely
```

Fix (we're already inside `if rebuild_dependencies { ... }`):

```rust
let file_path = Some(&*file_path);
```

## Bug 3 (FYI, not sure if by design): per-MCP-session dep state

`is_there_a_dependency_database(false)` returns `false` even right after I confirmed via log that `vanilla_files=685348` was assigned. Each MCP session looks to have its own `Arc<RwLock<Dependencies>>`. Claude Code opened sessions 2 and 3 in the same conversation (saw both in the server log), and the query landed on one that never had `set_game_selected` called on it. May be intentional isolation, but sharing read-only vanilla cache across sessions would be a nice improvement for agent clients. Not pushing this one, just flagging.

## Method

Patched HEAD at `background_thread.rs:572` (un-silence the error) and `Dependencies::rebuild` (log file_path branch, load result, `needs_updating`, and assignment). Built debug, copied cache files to `target\debug\dependencies\` (debug uses cwd-relative paths per `cfg!(debug_assertions)` in `settings.rs:543`), ran the sequence.

Feel free to ignore if you want and I can make a PR if you want.

To get the tsv files on the new format, you need to:

- Download RPFM 3.0.16 from the releases section.
- Download the schemas from the link I provided.
- Start RPFM 3.0.16 and do not update the schemas.
- Go to Game Selected/Open RPFM config folder, then in the folder that will open open the "schemas" folder (create it if it's missing), delete everything from it, and extract the schemas in there.
- Then restart RPFM 3.016, import all the tsvs you want into a Pack, and save it.
- Then open said pack in RPFM 4.0, and extract it.

That will leave you the extracted files you had using the new format. Of course, you can still use 3.0.16 instead, but keep in mind that no new schemas will be released for it.

https://github.com/esbmc/esbmc/issues/44:
## Diagnostic update: none of the remaining 10 KNOWNBUGs is fixable as a localized map / set / vector OM patch

After deep tracing of the four groups outside what's already in flight in #4422 and #4472, the residual KNOWNBUGs all bottom out in five distinct upstream causes — not in the STL operational models themselves. Splitting these out so the right people can pick them up independently:

| Group | Tests | Blocking issue |
|---|---|---|
| **B — map / multimap (StringClass keys)** | `map_string_class-3_bug`, `multimap_string_class_bug` | #4473 — `__memcpy_impl` loop is pruned by `assume(!guard)` at `--unwind 3 --no-unwinding-assertions`, masking assertions after any STL-of-struct mutation. Regressed from CORE to KNOWNBUG by #2133. |
| **B — map (`--ir` mode)** | `map_operator_brackets_bug` | #4474 — `--ir` flag forces Z3 integer/real and crashes with "operator is applied to arguments of the wrong sort" on char/string-keyed maps. |
| **A — list / template (string elements)** | `list_sort_bug`, `template/list_sort_bug`, `template/template_sort_string_bug` | Same root cause as #4473 — `std::string` operator copies hit the memcpy loop. Also depends on #4422 (node-based `std::list` rewrite) landing first. |
| **C — set (`--std c++03`)** | `set-value_comp-c++03` | #4475 — `src/cpp/library/utility` and `functional` use C++11+ syntax without `__cplusplus` guards. Sibling `set-value_comp-c++17` already passes. Filed in 2024 by Franz Brauße as `dup test, knownbug with --std c++03, success without` tracker. |
| **D — vector (capacity)** | `vector48` | #4476 — directly contradicts `vector28` (THOROUGH), which asserts the opposite. Cannot satisfy both with any deterministic capacity model. |
| **D — vector (leak)** | `vector67` | #4477 — vector's destructor is deliberately `#if 0`'d (per `d9f2ed3600`) because ESBMC shallow-copies temporaries, so re-enabling produces double-free. Affects all heap-owning OMs (vector, basic_string, valarray, auto_ptr). |
| **A — list / unique (doubles)** | `list_unique_bug-3` | The only string-free Group A test; should be tractable once #4422 (node-based `std::list`) merges. None of the above blockers apply. |

### Recommended sequencing

1. Land #4422 (node-based `std::list`, fixes 5 list tests) and #4472 (multiset insert return type, fixes 2 multiset tests).
2. After #4422, attempt `list_unique_bug-3` on top of the node-based list OM.
3. Pick up #4473 (memcpy-prune) — single highest-leverage fix; unblocks 5 of the remaining KNOWNBUGs across Groups A and B.
4. Pick up #4477 (vector destructor / temp shallow-copy) — affects multiple OMs beyond just this issue.
5. Resolve #4476 (delete one of vector28 / vector48) — admin cleanup.
6. #4475 (c++03 OM gating) is lowest priority — single tracker test for a fading standard.
7. #4474 (`--ir` Z3 sort error) is independent.

### Branch hygiene

I created `fix/4400-map-multimap-v2`, `fix/4400-set-value-comp-c++03`, and `fix/4400-vector` while investigating; all three have zero commits and will be deleted. The pre-existing placeholder branches `fix/4400-list-string-bugs` and `fix/4400-map-multimap` (also zero commits) should likely be removed too.

Hi @lwli1,

I apologize for the confusion - I misunderstood and thought the PPA version had resolved your issue. Let me help you properly rebuild ESBMC to fix the `uint32_t` problem.

**The root cause:** The issue is related to how ESBMC's frontend is configured with Clang. When built correctly with Clang 16, ESBMC will automatically find the C++ standard library headers.

Let's do a clean rebuild with the correct setup:

## Step 1: Clean previous build and set up project structure
We recommend creating an ESBMC_Project directory to organize the solvers.
```bash
# If you don't already have ESBMC_Project:
mkdir -p ESBMC_Project
cd ESBMC_Project

# If your esbmc directory is elsewhere, move it here:
# mv /path/to/esbmc .

# Clean previous build
cd esbmc
rm -rf build
cd ..
```

## Step 2: Install required dependencies
```bash
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install build-essential git gperf libgmp-dev cmake bison curl flex g++-multilib linux-libc-dev libboost-all-dev libtinfo-dev ninja-build python3-setuptools unzip wget python3-pip openjdk-8-jre libclang-cpp16-dev
```

## Step 3: Set up Clang 16 environment variables
```bash
apt-get install libclang-cpp16-dev &&
ESBMC_CLANG="-DLLVM_DIR=/usr/lib/llvm-16/lib/cmake/llvm -DClang_DIR=/usr/lib/cmake/clang-16" &&
ESBMC_STATIC=OFF
```

## Step 4: Set up Z3 solver (in ESBMC_Project directory)
```bash
wget https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/releases/download/z3-4.13.3/z3-4.13.3-x64-glibc-2.35.zip && unzip z3-4.13.3-x64-glibc-2.35.zip && mv z3-4.13.3-x64-glibc-2.35 z3
```

Your directory structure should now look like:
```
ESBMC_Project/
├── esbmc/
└── z3/
```

## Step 5: Configure and build ESBMC
```bash
cd esbmc && mkdir build && cd build

cmake .. -GNinja \
  $ESBMC_CLANG \
  -DBUILD_STATIC=$ESBMC_STATIC \
  -DZ3_DIR=$PWD/../../z3 \
  -DBUILD_TESTING=Off \
  -DENABLE_REGRESSION=Off \
  -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX:PATH=$PWD/../../release

cmake --build . && ninja install
```

## Step 6: Test with your code
```bash
./build/src/esbmc/esbmc  /path/to/your/minimal_example.cpp
```
It should work now.

https://github.com/esbmc/esbmc/issues/15:
> How can we rework the options/environment variables to allow
(a) specifying defaults for 32- and 64-bit sysroots and corresponding target triples, and
(b) keeping the regression tests as platform-independent as they are now?

Starting with clang-15.0.3 (and clang-16 as well), clang supports default configuration files, see e.g.
<https://blogs.gentoo.org/mgorny/2022/10/07/clang-in-gentoo-now-sets-default-runtimes-via-config-file/>. I think this would a good solution to the "user defaults" part of this problem. It would also make it Clang's burden to support those config files instead of our's.

It still doesn't solve the "generate correct target triple", though, but it's a first step - once we update our default clang frontend to 15.0.3 or later.

What we could do, is to replace the two huge assertions being generated by convert_typecast_to_ptr() by a single, smaller one, referring to the latest address-space array available at the point of the assertion. Currently it generates 2 assertions like this:
1. statically enumerate all addressed objects known at the time of converting `(void *)N` and for each of them check whether `N` is between `__ESBMC_ptr_obj_start_$obj` `__ESBMC_ptr_obj_end_$obj`, in which case the object-number is set to $obj and offset is computed as the difference between `N` and the start address of that symbol $obj.
2. If none of the above matched, the object must be the INVALID object.

Besides these two `__ESBMC_ptr_obj_(start|end)_$obj` symbols, we also maintain an array called `__ESBMC_addrspace_arr_$n` which contains at index $obj the same start/end address info (when $n > $obj). So the suggestion is (a) to use this addrspace array, like so:
```
(assert
 (let ((?addr_space_arr __ESBMC_addrspace_arr_$latest))
 (let ((?off (pointer_offset |smt_conv::smt_convt::int_to_ptr0|)))
 (let ((?obj (pointer_object |smt_conv::smt_convt::int_to_ptr0|)))
 (let ((?ptr (select ?addr_space_arr ?obj)))
 (let ((?addr (_ bv94182775894023 64)))   ; this is N in (void *)N
 (let ((?one (_ bv1 64)))
 (let ((?in (and (bvule ?addr (end ?ptr))
                 (bvuge ?addr (start ?ptr)))))
 (and (= ?off (bvsub ?addr (ite ?in (start ?ptr) ?one)))
      (or ?in (= ?obj ?one)))
))))))))
```
(b) to remove the INVALID entry from the addrspace array, and (c) to initialize the first addrspace array to have start = 1 and end = 0 (i.e., none of the entries match the ?in condition). Here, only `__ESBMC_addrspace_arr_$latest` needs to be updated to point to the lastest such array at the time the assertion is being made. This can be arranged by introducing a new symbol for each assertion which gets assigned the current addrspace array when the assertion is encoded.

https://github.com/esbmc/esbmc/issues/11:
According to my understanding the array-flattener (implemented by array_convt) is supporting infinite-size arrays by tracking store and select operations and deciding what term to encode based on that information. This results in larger formulas, because on every array update helper symbols need to be updated as well, but to my mind it is the only way to portably support those `constant_array_of2t` expressions for infinite-size arrays.

Now, as Mikhail said, the smtlib-backend already needs to use one of the tuple flatteners. The default, tuple-node-flattener, brings along its own copy of array_convt. So one option could be for smtlib to re-use that one also for infinite-size arrays, but that would lock out the other tuple-flattener. Also it would be smtlib-specific.

That's why I thought that maybe the managing layer smt_convt could keep an array_convt around when `!can_init_infinite_arrays` and use that for `constant_array_of2t` things...

> Update. With this resolved:
> > For >= v16 however, we need to solve #862 and, possibly, https://github.com/esbmc/esbmc/issues/1805 as well.
> 
> and with users starting to depend on Clang v15+ command line options like --gcc-install-dir (see <https://github.com/esbmc/esbmc/issues/1836#issuecomment-2148259781>), I agree with @rafaelsamenezes's [comment](https://github.com/esbmc/esbmc/pull/1810#issuecomment-2102361751):
> > IMO we should try to move forward into 24.04 since is the next LTS. It has from LLVM 14 up to 18.
> 
> and to go for either of the following:
> 1. v15: first version that supports --gcc-install-dir and last version that is fully compatible with ancient code, by which I mean the issues described in #862, or
> 2. v16: the _BitInt(n) with n > 128 won't be a problem anymore, but it enforces more strict -Werrors, which might be unexpected to users.
> 
> Going for 1. means least friction for users (out of the available options), while item 2. would mean a breaking change (similar to what Clang chose to do) for some users but would let us continue upgrading the Clang version in the future more easily.
> 
> @lucasccordeiro @rafaelsamenezes @mikhailramalho Do you have any opinions on this choice?
> 
> Personally, my preference would be v16: break now.

这属于系统bug：

> Media notifications associated with ongoing media playback must be exempt from the notification permission.
> https://source.android.com/docs/core/display/notification-perm

你先试试更新系统，如果依然不行应该跟手机厂商报bug；如果更新没解决跟我说一声，我等会做个声明需要（但不会主动申请）通知权限的版本让你先能用。

For the direct answer to your question, it's `ed20b176709c0e632400eed8d5bdb3abd510086719fe97fe9046b922f8faaf4f`. You can also obtain public keys of other reproducible F-Droid apps in the [metadata repo](https://gitlab.com/fdroid/fdroiddata) (for example, search for `AllowedAPKSigningKeys` in https://gitlab.com/fdroid/fdroiddata/-/blob/master/metadata/org.sunsetware.phocid.yml).

But I don't think that's a useful information to publish - what exactly are you verifying against when installing Phocid?

- If you trust the GitHub releases, GitHub provides SHA-256 hashes for the file.
- If you trust F-Droid, they have PGP signatures. 
- If you trust no platform but only me the developer - all communications originating from me could have been compromised by the platform, and my credentials could have been stolen, so you can't do that unless you know me in person.
- Updates to an already existing app must use the same signing key (or a new key specified by the previous keyholder), so manual certificate validation would be redundant.

I'll advise against blindly following Google's M3 Expressive ~guidelines~ marketing materials:

- At the time I originally created Phocid, 3 years after Material 3's original release and 1 year before Expressive, most of the basic widgets and styling are blatantly missing in Compose. I had to hack my own implementations ([what do you mean I had to use legacy Android components to look like the latest Android system?](https://github.com/TJYSunset/Phocid/blob/b16e7475a35aad434832fcde4146476073f32037/app/src/main/java/org/sunsetware/phocid/ui/theme/Animation.kt#L77)) which are less than ideal.
- Some of Expressive's new designs aren't cut out for music players. Take the [carousel](https://m3.material.io/components/carousel/guidelines) for example: not only you have to use a rectangular layout just to display a square image, but you're also displaying two pieces of cropped, irrelevant info. Heck, for players using non-deterministic shuffling, they can't even know the "previous" and "next" item in the carousel.
- This is my personal opinion, but [the current showcase on their website](https://kstatic.googleusercontent.com/files/d0a463d1d4b767b9a327739835737f23ee04e75e7e5835c2b6d657acc0ae6c046d3bb09fec5c169c20298a10ac74e04552d98c80aba4a82acce083ef0887f50e) is just horrendous.

I'd say just update the [easing functions](https://m3.material.io/styles/motion/overview/how-it-works) and add a shape morphing animation to the play/pause button would be enough "Expressive".

> New font as part of branding (I use FiraGO)

Although I think Fira is a readable font, I also strongly recommend an option to use the system font for accessibility reasons.

To me an offline music player can be offline in terms of the music source. Many like spotify because of spotify-connect casting. Although I do agree with you on the additional upkeep and updates for local networking.

Phocid should normally only start the service from the activity, which is not a background start.

Vinyl is using [the deprecated media library](https://github.com/VinylMusicPlayer/VinylMusicPlayer/blob/c84e73d5ae130df25b446128f4a02154cad85a3d/app/build.gradle#L138C43-L138C44) and Shuttle is using some [custom low-level solution](https://github.com/timusus/Shuttle/blob/42c0d6095e97ed6ac91df2be6c27f82ce60c053a/app/src/main/java/com/simplecity/amp_library/playback/MediaPlayerPlayback.kt#L9), so that looks like a bug in [media3](https://github.com/androidx/media). Unfortunately I can't do anything about bugs in Google's obscure code on specific hardware - you can try to raise a issue there and see if they could offer help.

In case you'd like to propose this, no, using a deprecated library or copying the custom solution from Shuttle is not feasible in the long run. I can't possibly keep up with all future Android updates and all the different hardware out there as a solo hobbyist.

Revisiting this issue several months later, I'm now sure the app-not-responding-on-start problem is caused by Phocid's approach on persisting library index; Only info of tracks themselves are stored, while derived info like artists and albums are constructed on startup. This has the advantage of drastically easier updates (not having to write migration code for every new feature added) and drastically less bugs (loading a bugged state is simply impossible), but is apparently not suitable if the library is obscenely large.

I hate to say this, but I don't think there's a solution to performance issues in large libraries; I could persist the other states as a **mitigation**, but they'll be forced to reconstruct eventually, and I'll have to write very unsound migration code to try to make that "eventually" doesn't mean "every new version" while introducing many bugs for the majority of users who doesn't even know this issue exist.

Anyway, this app is already dying unless Google's clown show gets stopped (and I'm pessimistic about that). You might want to start looking for alternatives regardless.

https://github.com/maincold2/Compact-3DG:
Oh, you've got a nice catch!
Previous works like VQVAE or VQGAN used not only codebook loss (embedding loss) but also commitment loss.
Even without commitment loss, codebooks can be trained through codebook loss.

In the recent codebase, codebooks were optimized through EMA rather than gradient descent, which was not intended (why vqloss was 0).
And, the Pytorch VQ package we used adopts in-place optimization (learn codebook in their module), and this is also the reason for 0 vqloss.

BTW, I've now updated the code by fixing the EMA to gradient descent for in-place codebook optimization.
Also, I've verified the performances are almost the same as before.

Thanks for bringing it to my attention!

Actually, the original ply file stores all parameters in 32 float containers, so it shows constantly high storage.
We now updated the code to save the result with npz format, reflecting the actual storage.
Please refer to the updated readme (https://github.com/maincold2/Compact-3DGS/tree/main?tab=readme-ov-file#442024).
Also, please check here for how to calculate the storage:
https://github.com/maincold2/Compact-3DGS/blob/50b48b0afe0205a834d479e3a0511068200d4409/scene/gaussian_model.py#L556

Thank you for your interest in our work and sorry for the late response.

We learn codebooks based on the activated attributes due to better performance.
And at the end of training, we apply VQ on the _scaling and _rotation parameters and save them with PLY.
This is because we reference unactivated parameters when rendering.

https://github.com/maincold2/Compact-3DGS/blob/a46a15ea8c600d8a24412871bae6da816dcb406e/scene/gaussian_model.py#L427-L434

As mentioned in the updated readme, colors are dependent on view direction, which is not supported by the original viewer.
Your saving code seems to show non-optimal performance because it always uses arbitrary direction ([0,0,1]).
We will try to support the proper viewer, and you can refer to other options in the readme at this time.

I have implemented something similar here:

https://github.com/BrianPugh/makerdiary-m60-config

The important bits are (might need some imports depending on how you organize your code):

```
from time import sleep

charger_in = digitalio.DigitalInOut(microcontroller.pin.P0_03)  # NOTE: low/False=charging, high/True=not_charging
charger_in.pull = digitalio.Pull.UP
def battery_charge():
    return not charger_in.value

# code.py
def macro_handler_batt(dev, is_down):  # invoke this in your macro_handler
    if is_down:
        is_charging = battery_charge()
        level = int(round(battery_level() / 7.14))
        dev.backlight.off()
        for i in range(level):
            if i == 0 and is_charging:
                dev.backlight.pixel(i, 255, 0, 0)
            else:
                dev.backlight.pixel(i, 0, 255, 0)

            if i != level - 1:
                sleep(0.03)

            dev.backlight.update()
    else:
        dev.backlight.set_mode(dev.backlight.mode)
```

So whenever you press this macro it does the following:
1. Turns off the backlight upon macro press.
2. Sets "esc" to red if charging, green otherwise.
3. Animates a bar along the top of your keyboard in green with the current charge level.
4. Restores the backlight upon macro release.

sudo xattr -r -d com.apple.quarantine /Library/Audio/Plug-Ins/Components/JS_Inflator.component

sudo codesign --force --sign - /Library/Audio/Plug-Ins/VST3/JS_Inflator.vst3  
sudo codesign --force --sign - /Library/Audio/Plug-Ins/Components/JS_Inflator.component

So we nearly had an example of this with #2316. I started to add `SOSToMILP` but then the `IndicatorToSOS` bridge would enable `Indicator` to `MILP`, except that `IndicatorToSOS` does not add variable bounds, so it would error.

To fix, we'd need a `final_touch` in `IndicatorToSOS` to update the final bounds on the slack variable, and then the usual final touch in `SOSToMILP`. So we'd have to touch `IndicatorToSOS` before touching `SOSToMILP`.

This will require a DAG of `final_touch` rather than the `Set` that we currently use, but it might be sufficient to just use a vector ordered by creation date?

1. kubegateway的graceful shutdown依赖底层http2 sever的graceful shutdown，之前由于依赖的apiserver版本中有部分graceful shutdown的bug fix commit未包含在内，所以graceful shutdown并未真正生效。通过这个PR已经解决了这个问题：https://github.com/kubewharf/kubegateway/pull/31

   http2 sever的graceful shutdown逻辑是在connection空闲时给长连接发送goaway，长连接断开的时机是不确定的，有时候也会出现大量空闲长连接在graceful shutdown开始时就立刻断开的情况。

2.  在生产实践中，我们部署了比较多的gateway实例，保证每个实例上的长连接影响范围较小，通过滚动更新策略保证每个实例之间的更新间隔时间。

通过以上两种方法，kubegateway某个实例异常或者重建时，长连接重建的影响可以在可控范围内

It does makes sense to maintain a separate ftdect here, like most of the other languages do (ex. javascript, ruby, rust, scala) because it's easier for users to update the plugin than it is to upgrade vim. I wrote a config to solve this problem for myself. Feel free to use it if you need it.

Known bugs:
* `Medium` operation mode doesn't work. `Low`, `High` and `Auto` works great.
* When water tank becomes empty, notifier sends a message. However, later the water seems to flow down the device's walls and an erroneous notification arrives, that the water tank have been refilled.
I didn't immediately notice these bugs and still haven't found the time to deal with them... =)

```yaml
fan humidifier:
  - platform: xiaomi_miio_airpurifier
    name: Xiaomi Air Humidifier
    host: <<IP_HERE>>
    token: <<TOKEN_HERE>>
    model: deerma.humidifier.jsq2w

switch:
  - platform: template
    switches:
      humidifier:
        friendly_name: Humidifier
        unique_id: <<RANDOM_UUID>>
        value_template: "{{ is_state('fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier', 'on') }}"
        turn_on:
          action: fan.turn_on
          target:
            entity_id: fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier
        turn_off:
          action: fan.turn_off
          target:
            entity_id: fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier
        icon_template: mdi:air-humidifier

      humidifier_buzzer:
        friendly_name: Buzzer
        unique_id: <<RANDOM_UUID>>
        value_template: "{{ is_state_attr('fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier', 'buzzer', True) }}"
        turn_on:
          action: xiaomi_miio_airpurifier.fan_set_buzzer_on
          data:
            entity_id: fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier
        turn_off:
          action: xiaomi_miio_airpurifier.fan_set_buzzer_off
          data:
            entity_id: fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier
        icon_template: mdi:volume-high

      humidifier_led:
        friendly_name: LED
        unique_id: <<RANDOM_UUID>>
        value_template: "{{ is_state_attr('fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier', 'led', True) }}"
        turn_on:
          action: xiaomi_miio_airpurifier.fan_set_led_on
          data:
            entity_id: fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier
        turn_off:
          action: xiaomi_miio_airpurifier.fan_set_led_off
          data:
            entity_id: fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier
        icon_template: mdi:led-outline

sensor:
  - platform: template
    sensors:
      humidifier_humidity:
        friendly_name: Humidity
        unique_id: <<RANDOM_UUID>>
        value_template: "{{ state_attr('fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier', 'humidity') }}"

        icon_template: mdi:water-percent

      humidifier_temperature:
        friendly_name: Temperature

        value_template: "{{ state_attr('fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier', 'temperature') }}"
        unit_of_measurement: "°C"

binary_sensor:

    sensors:
      humidifier_water_tank_detached:
        friendly_name: Water Tank Detached
        unique_id: <<RANDOM_UUID>>
        value_template: "{{ state_attr('fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier', 'no_water') }}" # attributes no_water and water_tank_detached are mixed up in the device!
        icon_template: >-
          {% if state_attr('fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier', 'no_water') %}
            mdi:cup-outline
          {% else %}
            mdi:cup-water
          {% endif %}
        device_class: problem

      humidifier_no_water:
        friendly_name: Water
        unique_id: <<RANDOM_UUID>>
        value_template: "{{ state_attr('fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier', 'water_tank_detached') }}" # attributes no_water and water_tank_detached are mixed up in the device!
        icon_template: >-
          {% if state_attr('fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier', 'water_tank_detached') %}
            mdi:water-off
          {% else %}
            mdi:water
          {% endif %}
        device_class: problem

input_select:
  humidifier_mode:
    name: Operation mode
    options:
      - Auto
      - Low
      - Medium
      - High

  humidifier_target_humidity:
    name: Target humidity
    options:
      - "30"
      - "40"
      - "50"
      - "60"
      - "70"
      - "80"

automation:
  - alias: Humidifier - select operation mode by input select
    trigger:
      entity_id: input_select.humidifier_mode
      trigger: state
    actions:
      action: fan.set_preset_mode
      data_template:
        entity_id: fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier
        preset_mode: "{{ states('input_select.humidifier_mode') }}"

  - alias: Humidifier - monitor operation mode and update input select
    triggers:
      trigger: template
      value_template: "{{ state_attr('fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier', 'preset_mode') }}"
    actions:
      action: input_select.select_option
      entity_id: input_select.humidifier_mode
      data_template:
        option: >
          {{ state_attr('fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier', 'preset_mode') }}

  - alias: Humidifier - select target humidity by input select
    triggers:
      trigger: state
      entity_id: input_select.humidifier_target_humidity
    actions:
      action: xiaomi_miio_airpurifier.fan_set_target_humidity
      data_template:
        entity_id: fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier
        humidity: >
          {{ states('input_select.humidifier_target_humidity') }}

  - alias: Humidifier - monitor target humidity and update input select
    triggers:
      trigger: template
      value_template: "{{ state_attr('fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier', 'target_humidity') }}"
    actions:
      action: input_select.select_option
      entity_id: input_select.humidifier_target_humidity
      data_template:
        option: >
          {{ state_attr('fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier', 'target_humidity') }}

  - alias: Humidifier - water is out notifier
    triggers:
      trigger: template
      value_template: "{{ state_attr('fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier', 'water_tank_detached') }}"
    actions:
      - action: <<NOTIFIER_ID_HERE>>
        data:
          message: "Humidifier: Water tank is empty"

  - alias: Humidifier - water refilled
    triggers:
      trigger: template
      value_template: "{{ not state_attr('fan.xiaomi_air_humidifier', 'water_tank_detached') }}"
    actions:
      - action: <<NOTIFIER_ID_HERE>>
        data:
          message: "Humidifier: Water have been refilled"
```

This is the way to go! This (custom) component was born at a time there didn't exist devices in Home Assistant. The architecture of Home Assistant has changed and evolved. This is the roadmap which should be tacked (but my time is the limiting factor here at moment):

1. Provide a platform called `xiaomi_miio` or `xiaomi_miio` at the lowest level of the `configuration.yaml` (`xiaomi_miio/__init__.py`).
2. Refactor the speed property. Only official supportes values are allowed here to be alexa/google home compatible. We have to move the speed / operation mode of the xiaomi device to a new service.
3. Use this platform to configure all supported devices. Expose `devices` and spawn multiple `entities` (fan, sensor, climate etc) as needed. Don't provide sensor values as attributes anymore.
4. Dispatch state updates received by `__init__.py` to all related entities (sensors, switches, etc.). 
5. Implement a config_flow to setup the platform via the UI. Tests are required. 

Do you like to join? ;-)

@liuhuaipu Could you provide some details about your device? Product name, model no. on the label, some specs. I want to use this information to update the list of supported devices as soon as the device is supported.

1. Protocol logic isn't allowed at the Home Assistant core. Every component requires a underlying library implementing the protocol.
2. No. `python-miio` is the implementation of the protocol plus an interface class per supported device. You can control every miio device by raw_commands via python-miio but this requires knowledge about the supported methods & properties. If a device class at python-miio is present (the device is listed as supported) the device can by controlled via well documented & speaking methods (on, off, status,...).
3. If we want airdog support at this custom component the python-miio device class implementation is required first / is the first step.
4. The commands are extracted from the Mi Home App (plugin).

Another question(s):

1. Could you explain the meaning of the "clean" property? What does "y(es)" and "n(o)" mean?
2. Could you provide a screenshot of the Mi Home app?
3. Could you provide a product name, model no. and some specs? This information is needed to updated the list of supported devices in future: https://github.com/syssi/xiaomi_airpurifier#supported-devices

wrote:

> mdadm has a "feature" that keeps it from mounting a raid drive if the
> hostname has changed -- https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=606481This
> _may_ cause trouble when stopping, then starting, a machine with a raid
> assembly -- We hit what I think is the bug above but now I can't recreate.
> 
> If this is a problem, the fix I'd like to make is to add 'HOMEHOST ignore'
> to the mdadm config file if no HOMEHOST is set. This will disable the
> check-for-same-machine feature of mdadm.
> 
> The volumes cookbook is expressly designed so that the node's metadata
> drives what's mounted, so I don't think the homehost-checking "feature"
> adds any value. An alternative would be to set the hostname to something
> stable, but any existing raid assemblies will have to be updated to match
> the new scheme.
> 
> I'm only going to make the patch if I see the error again, but I want to
> get Ops-y folks here to double-check the decision to disable HOMEHOST
> verification. If no negative opinions voiced I'll take that as go.

What do you mean under "old version"? @PaulStoffregen controls the versions of this library. The latest version is `1.1`. See http://platformio.org/lib/show/131/TimerOne/manifest

If your source code depends on the specific commit of this repo, you can use GIT URl with PlatformIO LIbrary Manager. See http://docs.platformio.org/en/latest/librarymanager/index.html

```
pio lib install https://github.com/PaulStoffregen/TimerOne.git
```

or in Project Configuration file
```ini
[env:mybuildenv]
board = teensy31
lib_deps = https://github.com/PaulStoffregen/TimerOne.git
```

P.S: I see that this repo contains 2 manifests: PIO + Arduino. Howerver, Arduino manifest is used. This library will be updated when new release will be created and `version` field will be modified.
https://github.com/syssi/xiaomi_fan/issu: You used to be able to set the angle using buttons, so you could tap them a couple of times to set the angle you want. Since Home Assistant updated the look of this page (quite a while ago), the buttons were replaced by a drop-down. I'm not sure if is was intended to choose only one direction in the first place, or that the Home Assistant developers did not think of such a use case. I'd say that either the integration or Home Assistant should be able to display them as buttons again. Since it's not an official integration, I'm not sure if Home Assistant would accept feedback about this, so I hope that the developer of this integration reads this and is able to modify the behavior.

## Findings: HomeKit Bridge + dmaker.fan.1c (Nature mode / Manual support)

I've successfully implemented Nature mode (Auto/Manual) support for the `dmaker.fan.1c` model via Apple HomeKit Bridge. Sharing our findings in case it's useful for the maintainers.

### Environment
- Model: `dmaker.fan.1c`
- Integration: `xiaomi_miio_fan` (syssi) via HACS
- Home Assistant with HomeKit Bridge integration
- `configuration.yaml` preset_modes_override: `["Nature"]`

### How HomeKit Bridge communicates fan mode

HomeKit exposes fans with a Manual/Auto toggle. The commands sent differ:
- **Auto** → `fan.set_preset_mode(preset_mode="Nature")`
- **Manual** → `fan.turn_on(percentage=X)` — note: NOT `set_preset_mode`. HA calls `async_turn_on(percentage, preset_mode, **kwargs)` meaning percentage arrives as the first positional argument (`speed`), not in `kwargs`.
- **Speed slider** → `fan.set_percentage(percentage=X)` — should preserve current mode, not change it

### All changes made to fan.py

1. `FAN_PRESET_MODES_1C` — added `"Nature": 0`
2. `FAN_SPEEDS_1C` — removed both `SPEED_OFF` and `"Nature"` from the list
3. `XiaomiFan1C.async_set_preset_mode` — added `"Nature"` handling via `async_set_natural_mode_on/off`
4. `XiaomiFan1C.async_set_natural_mode_on` — override: calls `self._device.set_mode(FanOperationMode.Nature)`
5. `XiaomiFan1C.async_set_natural_mode_off` — override: calls `self._device.set_mode(FanOperationMode.Normal)` and sets `self._natural_mode = False` on success. The parent class implementation does not work for 1C.
6. `XiaomiFan1C.async_set_percentage` — only sets speed via `self._device.set_speed()`, does NOT call `async_set_natural_mode_off`. Intentional — speed slider must preserve current mode.
7. `XiaomiFan1C.async_turn_on` — new override. Catches `percentage` from both positional `speed` arg and `kwargs`, calls `async_set_natural_mode_off` followed by `async_set_percentage`. This is the HomeKit "Manual" button handler.

### Critical insight: two separate code paths

```
HomeKit "Manual" button → fan.turn_on(percentage=X)
  → async_turn_on(speed=X)         ← positional arg!
  → async_set_natural_mode_off()   ← switches mode
  → async_set_percentage(X)        ← sets speed

HomeKit speed slider → fan.set_percentage(X)
  → async_set_percentage(X)        ← sets speed only, mode untouched
```

### Full async_turn_on implementation

```python
async def async_turn_on(
    self, speed: str = None, mode: str = None, **kwargs
) -> None:
    """Turn the device on."""
    await self._try_command(
        "Turning the miio device on failed.", self._device.on
    )
    self._state = True
    self._skip_update = True
    percentage = kwargs.get('percentage') or speed
    if percentage:
        await self.async_set_natural_mode_off()
        await asyncio.sleep(0.5)
        await self.async_set_percentage(int(percentage))
```

### Full async_set_natural_mode_off implementation

```python
async def async_set_natural_mode_off(self):
    """Turn the natural mode off."""
    if self._device_features & FEATURE_SET_NATURAL_MODE == 0:
        return
    result = await self._try_command(
        "Setting fan natural mode of the miio device failed.",
        self._device.set_mode,
        FanOperationMode.Normal,
    )
    if result:
        self._natural_mode = False
```

### Why asyncio.sleep(0.5)?
The 1C device needs a small delay between setting mode and setting speed, otherwise the speed command is ignored.

https://github.com/tohojo/flent/issues/1:
Rich Brown <notifications@github.com> writes:

>>  Basically, the problem is the way netperf determines its data output intervals in demo mode:
>
> Ahah! That was the explanation, but I wasn't (yet) smart enough to
> comprehend it. [To further test out my understanding... If netperf
> were to use a smaller "data output interval" when it's transmitting,
> it would create more frequent updates and the granularity would be
> higher...]
>
> But I'm not sure that fully explains it unless netperf uses a (very)
> different data output interval for receiving... The download chart has
> hundreds of points, while the ratio between my download and upload is
> about 10:1 (7mbps vs 768kbps) so I'd expect the upload chart to have
> something like dozens of points...

This is because netperf only checks whether it should output a data
point after it has sent a certain number of bytes. 16k in your case
(it's in the series metdata). At 768kbps, 16k bytes takes ~2.6 seconds
to send, so you'll get at most one data point for every such interval.

Now that I'm looking at this again, it seems that you can actually set
the send size. However, lowering it naturally impacts CPU usage. A quick
test on my laptop indicates that lowering it to 512 bytes raises
netperf's CPU usage from ~2% to ~20% for a single flow at half a
gigabit.

So yeah, we could probably lower it somewhat, but to what? We can't
know ahead of time what connection speed we will be running on.

If you want to try a quick test with a smaller size, this patch (to
Flent) adds the option unconditionally; obviously not the right way to
go about it, but it should allow you to test the impact at least:

diff --git a/flent/runners.py b/flent/runners.py
index a75223d..3d5e373 100644
--- a/flent/runners.py
+++ b/flent/runners.py
@@ -1049,6 +1049,7 @@ class NetperfDemoRunner(ProcessRunner):
                        "{marking} -H {control_host} -p {control_port} " \
                        "-t {test} -l {length:d} {buffer} {format} " \
                        "{control_local_bind} {extra_args} -- " \
+                       "-m 512,512 " \
                        "{socket_timeout} {local_bind} -H {host} -k {output_vars} " \
                        "{cong_control} {extra_test_args}".format(**args)

Update: You can also tell me to get a life :-) The change to the order of appearance of the results is far less important to me than substituting a nearby host for the -H host. Thanks

PS Thanks, too, for removing the "."

No, you won't be able to using this approach. If you have a look at the code, specifically:

[https://github.com/weavejester/lein-ring/blob/master/src/leiningen/ring/server.clj](https://github.com/weavejester/lein-ring/blob/master/src/leiningen/ring/server.clj)

you will see that lein-ring explicitly references the older org.clojure/tools.nrepl, and there is no way to override this without updating the code. You could use the forked version referenced above, i.e.:

[https://github.com/nxvipin/lein-ring](https://github.com/nxvipin/lein-ring)

or wait for an update.

Updates in what respect? Lein-Ring uses Clojure's reload mechanism, which won't work in the case where you're trying to overwrite an existing namespace alias. It doesn't currently make use of `refresh` in tools.namespace, with might solve the issue. A workaround is to run [ns-unalias](https://clojure.github.io/clojure/clojure.core-api.html#clojure.core/ns-unalias) manually.

https://github.com/foxyproxy/browser-ext:
v9.3
- Updated "Proxy by Patterns" to allow Tab Proxy only

Unfortunately, that is how browsers behave ATM and extensions can not do anything about it.

- Browser loads first
- Browser immediately starts network requests for its own functions (e.g. updates, configs)
- Browser starts loading extensions
- Browser starts loading the active tab and pinned tabs
- There is a race issue between loading extensions and the network connections

The only proxy configuration that is active on START-UP is PAC.

 ### Options
- Use a PAC
- Don't use Pinned tabs
- Set a blank tab as active tab when shutting down the browser

> > No, I just disabled it using the switcher next to the proxy name.
> 
> Disabling a proxy would be reflected in the sync. Therefore, the other machine will be set to a disabled proxy which is not possible, resulting in the mode to switch to disabled.
> 
> The concept of synchronising settings means the proxy-server settings will be the same on ALL machines.
> 
>     * If a proxy is disabled in one place, it will be disabled everywhere.
> 
>     * If a proxy is deleted in one place, it will be deleted everywhere.
> 
>     * If a proxy is added in one place, it will be added everywhere.
> 
>     * If a proxy is updated in one place, it will be updated everywhere.
> 
>

Several reasons for 16bit:
* The registers of the mcpwm module from esp32 are all 16bit.
* The code base between avr and esp32 have major portion of common code
* At max after ~4ms the current command is finished and the queue length is only filled up for approx. 10ms in order to be able to react to position, speed, acceleration changes nearly instantly. Consequently more than 16bit would make the driver just slow. This is a lessons learned from past driver incarnations: there was kind of 24bit values in use by a trick, and the driver just felt slow

As stated in the readme: do not block for long time the interrupts. And for esp32 in addition: do not write to the flash, while the stepper is running. For example, try to let the stepper run continuously and initiate an ota update. The system will work, but the stepper will run quite bumpy…..

Reason of the **Blocked aria-hidden** error in the console:

https://github.com/powdr-labs/powdr/issu:
I've investigated this idea a bit more, here is a small write-up.
I'm calling these basic block (BB) sequences `superblocks` (from literature).

generation:
- The prototype just saves the entire PC sequence from PGO execution, then iterates through it keeping a "window" of the last few BBs executed (clearing the window on an invalid BB).
  As the window is modifided, we update the superblock counts.
- This could also be done online during PGO execution, but would need passing info about the original BBs to PGO.
- Superblock instructions are just a concatenation of the original BBs instructions.
  This then goes through APC generation.
  Superblocks also include extra information about instruction indexes and PCs.
  BBs only need the `start_pc`, but superblocks need one `(instruction idx, pc)` tuple for each of the original BBs.
- `statements_to_symbolic_machine` now adds `pc_lookup` constraints for each of the `(idx, pc)` tuples, instead of just the first instruction and PC.
  Is this enough to constrain a valid superblock execution or do we need more for soundness?

superblock sizes:
- Increasing the number of BBs allowed in superblocks doesn't increase the number of superblocks much (its basically linear with the number of original BBs).
  I think the main issue is the size of these superblock constraint systems, which might slow down APC geneneration quite a bit.
- Maybe we can already discard some of these superblocks based on execution frequency and size (before APC gen)?

superblock optimization:
- Should we look for missing optimization opportunities?

PGO superblock selection:
- we want to choose superblocks/BBs based on their benefit to execution.
- estimating "how good" a superblock is by itself is simple (same as for an original BB).
  The issue is, when we pick a superblock because it is the most benefitial, we should lower the benefit of the "conflicting" superblocks and BBs (assuming we allow multiple APC choices for execution).
  That seems somewhat tricky?
- Consider we choose `abc` (each letter is an original BB).
  We should discount its frequency from every "contained" APC: `a, b, c, ab, bc`.
  What about APCs including `abc` (e.g., `abcd` and `dabc`)? I guess they can be completely discarded?
  What about APCs with common affixes (e.g., `ea`, `ce`)? We probably want to discard them too: at least for `ea`, we don't want to pick it during execution if `abc` follows.

some experiments on the keccak example and reth (with size 2 superblocks):
- Most popular superblocks are loop related (as expected), some are actual unrolling (e.g., `aa`), but I also see some cycles, e.g., `ab`/`ba` and `ab`/`bc`/`ca`, the latter here might benefit from larger superblocks.
- I manually compared superblock effectivenes with the effectiveness of just its first BB (so not quite exact!).
  It looks somewhat promising:
  - for some keccakf superblocks I saw 10-20% more effectiveness
  - for some memcpy superblocks I saw 10-50% more effectiveness

@guilima I actually use a whole new function in my themes now I created from scratch that actually uses attachment ID's to process the image instead of a url which means you can update the attachment meta to include the cropped image as a new size hence fixing issues with CDN's and image deletions, maybe something to consider doing for your own site, it's a fairly easy tweak if you are modifying the aq class.

See the following core functions:

- wp_get_attachment_metadata()
- get_attached_file()
- wp_update_attachment_metadata()
- wp_get_attachment_image_src() - always better to return your images passed through the wp_get_attachment_image_src' filters as well.

So instead of parsing an image URL send an attachment ID to the class and use the functions listed above to get the image path, crop things and once cropped use wp_update_attachment_metadata() to update the image metadata to add your custom size to the list if sizes in the array, then return your new image via wp_get_attachment_image_src().
https://github.com/hashicorp/next-mdx-en:
Right now, restart the watcher.

This plugin is most useful in concert with [babel-import-glob-array](https://github.com/jescalan/babel-plugin-import-glob-array), which is cached by babel, but when you add a new file the babel cache doesn't update since it's watching the file contents, not the external filesystem. There are a few issues open for this that I am tracking, but at the moment there's no concrete solution.

Additionally, there is a ton of overhead that comes with scanning the full file tree on every change to every file. We were unable to find a way to get webpack to tell us specifically that a new file has been added, and not an existing file changed etc, so there are only two options: scan the file tree every time there's a change to anything, which is slow, or scan only on initial run. We default to only the initial run, but that can be disabled via [this parameter](https://github.com/hashicorp/prebuild-webpack-plugin#clearcacheonupdate) to the underlying dependency. The performance hit is so significant though, that we made it so that you have to explicitly enable it. This could be solved if we/anyone could figure out how to get from webpack that specifically a new file has been added - the only way I have seen this done elsewhere is caching every file in the project and whenever a change comes in comparing it against the previous state to determine if the file exists, which has overhead as well. But very much open to contributions there.

If you're not using the import glob array plugin, this problem can be solved but with a large perf hit by adding an option to change the file tree scan frequency to the prebuild plugin, and could be solved with less of a perf hit if anyone can figure out how to get webpack to tell you whether a changed file is newly added or not within a plugin. If you are using the import glob array plugin, we would also need to solve the babel caching issue on top of that in order for everything to work smoothly.

In the interest of continuing to move forward, and given the speed of a watcher restart and the infrequency of new file adds in the middle of a dev server watch, we basically decided to table this.
As a note, if you are using the import glob array plugin, you'll want to clear out the babel cache before each dev mode run with something like `rm -rf node_modules/.cache/babel-loader/` - which you can build right into your npm command in `package.json`.

Hopefully this helps, and sorry this isn't 100% as smooth as could be!

https://github.com/stillwater-sc/univers:
## Attempted the sqrt-Newton fix -- DISPROVEN (sqrt is 308-digit accurate, not under-resolving)

Followed up on candidate fix (1) ("make sqrt resolve past the 19-block plateau"). It is a dead end, with hard data (PR #1180 updates the diagnostic accordingly):

**sqrt(0.75) is not the bottleneck.** Instrumenting the Newton iteration against a correct 320-digit reference:
```
iter 0 (d= 2): blocks= 2 accuracy= 33 digits
iter 1 (d= 4): blocks= 4 accuracy= 65
iter 2 (d= 8): blocks= 8 accuracy=129
iter 3 (d=16): blocks=16 accuracy=260
iter 4 (d=20): blocks=19 accuracy=309   <- converges quadratically to 308-309
```
sqrt(0.75) is **308-digit accurate** at its 19-block floor, and stays 308 at depth 20..40. The plateau is the **~2^-1022 double-host precision floor**, not an under-resolution -- sqrt cannot go deeper on a double host. (`div_online` in the Newton step is strictly *worse*: 224. My earlier "sqrt might be under-resolving" framing, and the "block 18 under-resolved" reading, are both refuted; sqrt/asin are accurate.)

**The decisive evidence it's the series, not the argument** -- truncate the argument to k blocks, take sin:
```
 k    14  15  16  17  18  19
 pi6 231 247 264 280 297 306    (each deep block adds ~16 digits -- USABLE)
 asin 231 234 234 234 234 234   (blocks 15..19 add NOTHING -- UNUSABLE)
```
asin agrees with pi/6 to 306 and sin is well-conditioned at pi/6, so `sin(asin)` *ought* to reach ~306. It stalls at 234 because the sin Maclaurin recurrence **cannot extract the precision that lives in asin's deep-block decomposition** (blocks 15-19 are dead weight to it). So the fix is not in sqrt/asin/div/atan -- all accurate -- but in the sin/cos series machinery's conditioning against a non-prefix deep tail.

**Remaining candidate fixes** (both non-trivial):
- (a) a sin/cos series summation that does not compound the deep-block error term-to-term (the hard, real fix);
- (b) a wider intermediate host for the inverse-trig -> sin round-trip so the deep tail sits above the float floor with margin.

The diagnostic test (`asin_sqrt_precision.cpp`) now carries the convergence trace + truncation sweep and its LEVEL_4 characterization locks sqrt's 308-digit accuracy, so this disproven hypothesis can't silently return.
