你好 @ilikik，感谢反馈 🙏 这个能力已经实现并发布啦。

确实如你所说，**httpOnly 的 cookie 没法用 JS（`document.cookie`）读/改/删**——所以新工具没走页面 JS，而是直接调浏览器引擎的 `nsICookieManager` 操作 cookie 存储，**httpOnly 也能管**。

### 新增内置工具 `cookies`（单个工具，用 `action` 区分）
- **`action: "list"`** —— 列出 cookie（可按 `domain` / `name` 过滤），**含 httpOnly**（普通 `document.cookie` 看不到的）；
- **`action: "set"`** —— 新增 / 修改（支持 `httpOnly` / `secure` / `sameSite` / 有效期 `expires`(unix 秒) 或 `maxAge`(相对秒)；同 host+path+name 即覆盖，写后会读回确认）；
- **`action: "remove"`** —— 删除（`name`+`domain` 删单个 / 只给 `domain` 删该 host 下全部 / `all: true` 清空所有）。

底层经引擎 `nsICookieManager`，可跨域、可管理登录态。

### 已发布
👉 [**v0.21.0**](https://github.com/WhiteNightShadow/firefox-reverse/releases/tag/v0.21.0)，三端（macOS / Windows / Linux）安装包都已更新，**重新下载安装即可**（本版内部 build ID 已更新，重装会自动刷新缓存，不用手动清）。

README 的「工具大全」也按你的建议加了一栏 **🍪 Cookie 管理** 👍

### 用法（在 Agent 侧边栏用自然语言让它调）
> 用 cookies 工具把 example.com 下名为 `sid` 的 httpOnly cookie 删掉
> 给 example.com 新增 cookie：`token=abc`，httpOnly，有效期 1 小时

https://github.com/jamesmunns/bbqueue/is:
Hi @OpoOpo, at the moment, I don't think this is possible. The lock-free algorithm used by bbqueue currently expects there to only be one Consumer and one Producer, which each have tracking variables they are allowed to update.

Allowing the producer to overwrite the consumer's data would (currently) invalidate this, leading to potential race conditions.

If you think you know a way to correct this, I'd be happy to discuss!

Hey Andrew,

For a variety of reasons, bbqueue only handles u8s. This is largely due to the ability to avoid things like alignment or other issues.

If this is a problem, I'd probably suggest checking out `heapless`'s Queue structures (spsc or mpmc).

I'll leave this issue open as a reminder to update the docs to make this more clear!

2016-09-15 14:47:18 INFO:req [7g4is1zglxsso] [I->M] onMessage: irc.freenode.net from=ton31337 (null@irc.freenode.net) to=#prometheus
```

It looks like any message claimed by `murtaza1983[m]1` gets duplicated, but messages claimed by `M-daodennis` are not.

`murtaza1983[m]` does seem to have 2 instances (one has the `1` suffix, but their user IDs are distinct), but it looks like we claim based off nick - https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-appservice-irc/blob/develop/lib/irc/IrcEventBroker.js#L165 - Both clients will desire the same nick, but they should've been clobbered and updated correctly. If, hypothetically, this didn't happen, then you would have 2 clients which think they have the same nick, and if they are the claimer then they would send the message through twice (once f.e message received down their TCP connection). I suspect that is what is happening here.

If you're using Anaconda, you'll need to run ktUpdateTaxonomy.sh in your environment. The same goes for Miniconda you may need to create two folders, `taxonomy` and `scripts`, in the '~/miniconda3/envs/krona_env/bin' directory. This is what I did, and then I linked the scripts and content from the `~/miniconda3/envs/krona_env/opt/krona/scripts` folder `ln -s ~/miniconda3/envs/krona_env/opt/krona/scripts/taxonomy.make .`  `ln -s ~/miniconda3/envs/krona_env/opt/krona/scripts/extractTaxonomy.pl .`. Finally, run ktUpdateTaxonomy to complete the process.
 – if you encounter an error. 
navigate to cd `~/miniconda3/envs/krona_env/opt/krona/taxonomy/` and run `./updateTaxonomy.sh`. This will update the taxonomy and resolve the issues.

https://github.com/jseidl/magic-areas/is:
Yes that's correct, I have no sleep lights defined in the kitchen or landing, there was some additional chat with Claude that I didn't bother to include as it didn't add anything new but here it is:

> >would this also apply if the sleep lights group is empty?
> 
> ⏺ Yes, absolutely! The race condition would still apply even if the sleep lights group is empty. Here's why:
> 
>   The Logic Flow:
> 
>   1. When sleep mode is enabled, the area gets a SLEEP state added to self.area.states
>   2. The overhead lights group checks if any priority states (like SLEEP) are active
>   3. If priority states are active, overhead lights should NOT turn on (lines 398-400)
>   4. But due to the race condition, the overhead lights group might check has_priority_states before the SLEEP state is visible in self.area.states
>   5. It concludes "no priority states" and turns on the overhead lights
> 
>   Key Code:
>    light.py lines 359-364
>   has_priority_states = any(
>       self.area.has_state(state) for state in AREA_PRIORITY_STATES
>   )
> 
>    lines 398-400
>   if has_priority_states:
>       for non_priority_state in non_priority_states:
>           valid_states.remove(non_priority_state)
> 
>   The Issue:
>   - AREA_PRIORITY_STATES = [AREA_STATE_SLEEP, AREA_STATE_ACCENT] (const.py line 423)
>   - Overhead lights are non-priority states
>   - When SLEEP is active, overhead lights should be removed from valid_states and not turn on
>   - But the race condition means the check happens before SLEEP is visible
> 
>   Empty Sleep Lights Group:
>   - If sleep lights are empty, no sleep light group is created (lines 82-90 in light.py)
>   - But the overhead lights group still exists and still checks for priority states
>   - The race condition still causes it to turn on when it shouldn't
> 
>   So the fix needs to ensure that when checking for priority states, the check is atomic with the state update, regardless of whether a sleep lights group exists.

Hi, a few questions:
1. Does this only happens on registry updates? (just to make sure you're commenting on the right bug)
2. There are a few group types that will turn on all the other lights when one of the lights turn on (this is not magic areas) like zigbee groups. I'm not sure if Home Assistant's regular groups do the same. Are you using those?
https://github.com/second-state/wasm-lea:
This update is actually a really cool feature i.e. hotswap the logic of a program in real-time. 

From a security standpoint:

a) the update requires that the `SSVM_Admin_Key` is in the headers. Only the original creator knows this key

b) from an integrity standpoint the frontend can also be written to check the `wasm_sha256` of the binary each time it calls the backend. Please see example below.

```
curl --location -g --request GET 'https://rpc.ssvm.secondstate.io:8081/api/executables/498?filterBy=[%22wasm_sha256%22]'
```

Notice how the checksum is the same as the checksum returned (above) when the original wasm binary was deployed to the FaaS? If the Rust is changed in anyway the checksum will be completely different.

```
{"wasm_sha256":"0x4c1dc9592520448bc24cf37e6851814d43619f62a0be72bda607af61799f1b26"}
```

This means that a front end can always be 100% sure that it is calling logic that it trusts. With this extra security step the application developer would update the logic and then make sure the front end is aware of the new checksum.

        platform: number

        state_class: "measurement"
        uom: "W"

        registers: [0x1024]
        range:
          min: 0
          max: 65535
        writeback:
          register: 0x1023
          count: 2
```

Update after applying suggested YAML changes (PV sensors removed):

Following your suggestion, I removed the entire PV group from the profile
(deye_hybrid.yaml), as well as PV-related production sensors.

After this change:
- Entities started receiving values
- Communication and polling clearly work
- However, most values do not match real-world data and appear incorrectly
  scaled or mapped

Important note about the hardware:
The AE-FS2.0-2H2 Balcony ESS **does support 2 PV inputs**, although in my specific
installation no PV panels are currently connected. So PV-related registers may
still exist on the device, but likely follow a different layout or scaling.

> > But it will be overwritten on next update. It would be much better to change it from gui.
> 
> **Solution for definition file not being overwritten:**
> 
> * use File Browser and go to your folder "inverter_definitions"
> * add a folder called "custom"
> * copy definition file into the custom folder and edit it for your needs
> * choose the edited file as your definition file
> 
> ... this newly created definition file won't be overwritten by the next update.
> 
> Have fun ☀️

I updated the settings referring to `${workspaceFolder:...}` to just use `${workspaceFolder}` since it is more robust against the project's root directory being renamed. If people want to use multi-root workspaces they can update the `launch.json` themselves I think (the reason the Swift extension uses the `${workspaceFolder:...}` syntax is that it has functionality to update `launch.json` accordingly when a project's root directory is renamed).

Again, thanks for the config file examples! I've never used CodeLLDB myself so it might've taken me a bit longer to figure it out.

Now, numa_distance will initialize the global variable distance_table to speed up the next query, and it will allocate memory.

But i have two ideas：
1. Should we provide a one-time query interface, like `numa_distance_once`, and update the description of numa_distance?
2. numa_distance has concurrency issues, which may cause address loss and memory leaks. we should use atomic operations for assignment.

https://github.com/flyte/mqtt-io/issues/:
I also had this problem, but I've been able to come up with what seems to be a workaround.

In general, I would very much agree with these quoted coments from @tkislan; with the runtime dependency installation, I think the app needs some way to know that it needs a board/GPIO specific library (`RPi.GPIO`) for a `sensor_module` so that custom Docker Images or extra unused configs (my workaround) aren't required.

EDIT: I had some further thoughts on this; the DHT22 Library from AdaFruit seems to be using some kind of Board detection library to know to import the RPi.GPIO module. If this could be used, I think that would make the options:
* Provide some way to do this via configuration
* Include the Board Detection library as a dependency, call it and `pip install` the appropriate library
* Some kind of two-stage runtime dependency setup, where the first stage installs the Sensor Libtaries (the DHT22 library in my example case), and then does a second pass to see if *those* libraries have any runtime dependencies that need to be installed too (like `RPi.GPIO`).

> │   raspberrypi:                                                                                                                  │
> │   - GPIO module 'raspberrypi' does not have any digital_input or digital_output configs
> ```
> 
> and if I remove it, I get an error that it can't import RPi GPIO
> 
> ```

> ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'RPi'
> ```

> 
> I would like to avoid building a custom Docker image just for this

# Workaround

There are two parts to my workaround; the initial issue where `RPi.GPIO` isn't available, and then once that is working some other permissions issues with Docker

## Can't import `RPi.GPIO`

For the workaround I:
* Added the `raspberrypi` GPIO Module as @tkislan mentions
* Added a `digital_output` on a GPIO Pin that I'm not using, to get around the error about not having input or output configs

This leaves a mqtt-io config.yml like so (I haven't tried adding `ha_discovery` yet, but I will be needing that):
```
gpio_modules:
  - name: rpi
    module: raspberrypi

digital_outputs:
  - name: gpio0
    module: rpi
    pin: 0

sensor_modules:
  - name: dht22_sensor
    module: dht22
    type: dht22
    pin: 4

sensor_inputs:
  - name: temperature
    module: dht22_sensor
    interval: 10
    digits: 4
    type: temperature

  - name: humidity
    module: dht22_sensor
    interval: 10
    digits: 4
    type: humidity
```

## Docker permissions

With that in place, I then get some further errors from the GPIO Library saying that it can only be run on a Raspberry Pi, or `/dev/mem` can't be accessed  and to try running as root.

I ended up with a service like this in my docker-compose.yml
```
  gpio:
    image: flyte/mqtt-io
    cap_drop:
      - ALL  # DHT22 via Pi GPIO seems to run fine without any CAPs
    security_opt:
      # Needed to access /proc/device-tree/system/linux,revision
      # So the GPIO Library can confirm it's running on a Pi
      # This could allow a process in the container to write to /proc, if it can elevate
      # But I think this should still be better than privileged mode
      # Maybe apparmor could be used to lock things down more precisely?
      - systempaths=unconfined
    group_add:
      # Add the mqtt_io user (that the process runs as) to the Host GPIO Group (provided by env var here)
      # Without this, filesystem permissions inside the container will prevent access to GPIO
      - ${GPIO_GID}
    volumes:
      - ${CONFIG_DIR}/gpio.yml:/config.yml
    devices:
      #- /dev/gpiochip0  # might be needed to access the Pi GPIO, but seems OK commented out
      - /dev/gpiomem  # Needed for the GPIO Library to check for GPIO access
```

If I get some time, I will try to raise a PR with some updates to the Doc page about running in Docker.

```

Hey, I've been using mqtt-io for a few days now to count pulses of my gas meter and publish those to home assistant. So far, I'm using a custom little automation in HA to count the pulses. It works, but it's of course not a really good solution regarding missing pulses due to reboots of HA after updates etc. 

So, I thought to myself, how hard can it be to add this. I'm quite experienced in a few programming languages - however, not including python. And so it turned out to be harder than I anticipated. 😬 

Having a look at [server.py](https://github.com/flyte/mqtt-io/blob/6297a22f1200a35f890c2487428ee84ab2c671cd/mqtt_io/server.py#L357), I think one could rather easily add the incrementing in the definition of the callback. An easy way to retain the state would be a local file. Extending the config (specifically `self.digital_input_configs`) with  
- a filepath,
- an initial value (in case the file does not exist yet), 
- the incrementation per pulse and
- pulses to accumulate before publishing to MQTT

should do the trick. 
That's of course only a very basic implementation (without the cool MQTT config stuff) but miles ahead of losing steps on the receiving end due to a multitude of reasons...

I am also in need of PWM, so I was looking at implementing this, but I'm not sure what the best approach is. I'm not sure if I'll be able to find the time to actually implement this, but in the interest of moving this forward, here are some considerations I had while looking at the code for this. Feedback is welcome.

 - One big question is whether this should be handled by the GPIO modules, or some new type of module (like sensors, but then for output). GPIO seems the easiest approach, since that allows reusing a lot of setup and event handling code. However, that works for modules like beaglebone and (I guess) raspberry pi that (I think) talk to hardware directly to set up PWM, but might be less suited to more generic modules. e.g I think libgpiod and `/dev/gpiochipx` has no way to configure PWM output. There is a `/sys/class/pwm/pwmchipx` interface, but that does require devicetree configuration to let the PWM driver take control of pins rather than runtime configuration. Of course, it can be acceptable that PWM output is supported only by some modules, and there could mabye even be a pwmchip-sysfs gpio module that *only* supports PWM output (to be used together with the gpiod module for regular GPIO for example).
 - If handled by GPIO modules, configuration can just be done like digital_outputs ([see docs](https://github.com/flyte/mqtt-io/wiki/GPIO-Configuration#digital_outputs)), but some `supports_pwm` or `enable_pwm` directive needs to be added. I guess PWM-enabled pins can still also be controlled as regular digital pins (e.g. by passing `on_payload` as value instead of a PWM value), so all regular values can still apply as normal. 
 - Normal digital GPIO is now handled by `digital_output_loop()` (that converts `on_payload/off_payload` to a bool) and `set_digital_output()` (that logs and passes the value to the right gpio module). The easiest approach would be to generalize these methods to just pass either booleans or numbers, and let the gpio module call the right output method based on the type of value.
 - An alternative approach that is a bit more explicit is to add `set_pwm_output()` next to `set_digital_output()` and add a `set_pwm_pin()` to gpio modules, letting `digital_output_loop()` call the right method based on the type of value.
 - One step more explicit would be to use a different topic for PWM values, e.g. something like `<topic_prefix>/output/<name>/set_pwm` (which saves the "look at the content to decide whether to do PWM or on/off", which is a bit fragile). This would probably be implemented by letting `_handle_digital_output_msg` match the `set_pwm` topic and if so, adding a different kind of message to the gpio queue (maybe adding a type flag or so) so `digital_output_loop()` (which processes the loop) can dispatch to the right method. This approach is probably the most explicit and most appropriate, without needing much extra boilerplate code.
 - A bit off-topic, but looking at the code, I wonder a bit about the code split between `_handle_digital_output_msg()` and `digital_output_loop()`. The former looks at the topic and handles `set_on_ms` and `set_off_ms` directly by dispatching a task, and puts regular pin updates in a queue that is handled by `digital_output_loop()` (which also matches the payload against `on_payload`/`off_payload` config). For the `set_on/off_ms` handling, the comments say this is done in a task to prevent blocking `digital_output_loop()`, but the latter also handles the `timed_set_ms` configuration by *also* dispatching a task, essentially duplicating the `set_on_ms` code in a different place. I suspect these different timed features might have been introduced over time, but looking at them now, it seems this code could be refactored to be simpler.

https://github.com/dsherret/ts-nameof/is: @PKnight-CheckWriters I updated the main post to show some examples.

@bzhu94 in your case:

1. Install `ts-nameof`, `@types/ts-nameof`, and `ttypescript` as dev dependencies.
2. Set the `TS_NODE_COMPILER` environment variable to `ttypescript` when running your script.

For example, I think you could do that with the `cross-env` package by doing:

```
cross-env TS_NODE_COMPILER="ttypescript" mocha -r ts-node/register Tests/**/*.tsx Tests/**/*.ts
```

(save cross-env as a dev dependency from npm)

You should be able to remove all the type reference directives (`/// <reference />`) if you install the `@types/ts-nameof` package. Generally that package or the type reference directive is only needed to get type checking to work and it doesn't have any affect on runtime.

3. Update your *tsconfig.json* to have a "plugins" field. ttypescript will use this to inject ts-nameof as a transformer for the compiler to use.

```json
{
    "compileOnSave": true,
    "compilerOptions": {
        "target": "es6",
        "jsx": "react",
        "noImplicitAny": false,
        "removeComments": false,
        "declaration": false,
        "noEmitOnError": true,
        "sourceMap": false,
        "moduleResolution": "node"
    },
    "include": [ "./**/*.ts", "./**/*.tsx"],
    "exclude": [ "./node_modules" ],
    "plugins": [{ "transform": "ts-nameof", "type": "raw" }] 
}
```

At the time of this writing, Audio processing can only occur in the browser's main thread. This will require modifications to the Love code to perform audio state updates in the main thread.

An alternative option could be [libopenmpt](https://lib.openmpt.org/libopenmpt/), which is actually maintained and updated, and has nearly the same feature set (to my knowledge, except some functions that löve didn't make use of anyway.)

`setVolume` is definitely working. If you try out this code you'll see that the volume gradually goes down. I don't think I can make any guarantees about relative loudness in the browser compared to other platforms. Maybe someone will have some insight into this but best can I suggest right now is to check `love.system.getOS()` or `love._os` for the text "Web" and make your source less loud if it's in a browser.

``` lua
local snd = 0
local timer = 1.5
volume = 1

function love.load()
    snd = love.audio.newSource('Jump19.wav')
end

function love.update(dt)
    timer = timer + dt

    if timer >= 1.5 then
        volume = volume - .1
        snd:setVolume(volume)
        timer = 0
        snd:play()
    end
end

```

In practice, however, sbt-pack is very tiny plugin, and for the maintainability its release process should not depend on the other projects. 

For example, version number ordering strategy is often subjects to change (for example, if the user use 0.1-(git revision number) tag, we need to extend the ordering, but to modify this we need to update both of sbt-jars and sbt-pack and deploy them to sonatype. It doubles the operation cost.

Having packAllLibJars task in sbt-pack plugin is a nice idea. I prefer to add this task to sbt-pack.

Thanks.

https://github.com/soft-matter/trackpy/i:
I opened issue #700 at the time. It would be better if OP could provide a minimal reproducible example. For what it's worth, I tried replicating the error with the trackpy-example repository.

Python: 3.10 / 3.12 (tested on two different venvs)
trackpy: 0.7.0
pims: 0.7.0
OS: Windows 11

```py
import pims
import trackpy as tp

frames = pims.ImageSequence('sample_data/foam/*.tif')

tp.quiet()  # Disabling progress reports makes this a fairer comparison

tp.batch(frames[:100], 13, invert=True, processes=3)
```

When launching the script like this, as stated by OP there's a bunch of neverending errors which boil down to always the same message:

```
RuntimeError:
        An attempt has been made to start a new process before the
        current process has finished its bootstrapping phase.

        This probably means that you are not using fork to start your
        child processes and you have forgotten to use the proper idiom
        in the main module:

            if __name__ == '__main__':
                freeze_support()
                ...

        The "freeze_support()" line can be omitted if the program
        is not going to be frozen to produce an executable.
```

Now, if you run the following script instead:

```py
import pims
import trackpy as tp

if __name__ == "__main__":
    frames = pims.ImageSequence('sample_data/foam/*.tif')
    tp.quiet()  # Disabling progress reports makes this a fairer comparison
    features = tp.batch(frames[:100], 13, invert=True, processes=3)
    print(f"Found {len(features)} features in the first 100 frames.")
```

The problem goes away. I think that pims is automatically switching to using tifffile to read the images so in the end the problem is that when using multiprocessing.Pool (which is called in tp.batch), unless you specify the execution within an `if __name__ == "__main__"` condition, it'll never work.

It is [fork's fault](https://stackoverflow.com/a/20361032/4437552) that makes using the idiom required.

EDIT: this problem occurs only on scripts; when using jupyter notebooks the code works fine even without `__main__`
EDIT: updated to notify that i also tested on python 3.12 to have consistency

This is a great idea! The person who implements it will probably have to be you or someone else who is motivated to use it for their research. Trackpy does some of the work already; you can write a class that inherits `tp.predict.NullPredict` and keeps a buffer of positions in the last n frames (the way that `tp.predict._RecentVelocityPredict` does). Then you will need to loop over each particle to update the filter. 

The next part will be making it fast, if you have a lot of particles. There's a wider community who can help with that. Speaking generally, for this part it helps tremendously to have some tests that validate that the Wiener filter prediction is working correctly, which makes it possible to for a non-expert to quickly check whether their optimized code is valid.

Hi @Altairch95,

If you're going to edit some of those functions, the best way is to fork the trackpy repository. That way (a) you can track and manage the relationship between your code and the "official" version as we release updates or bug fixes; (b) if you want to prepare a contribution to the "official" version, it's much easier.

Because this is a much more general problem, and there are a lot of potential outputs, another option you should consider is to write separate function(s) to characterize particles after they are located. Those tend to be easier to maintain, and can give users more flexibility because there can be as many arguments and outputs as needed, whereas we tend to be very conservative about adding to the `locate()` API.

I'll keep you updated!

Basically, it's out of scope for this ansible role. It's meant to bootstrap and update a cluster, not to deploy all sorts of stuff on it. When you include this kind of thing in your bootstrap, you risk having all sorts of problems. The easy solution imo is to deploy flux operator with the bootstrap and have it point to a git repo where you then deploy the CRD and update Cilium, like in the docs. Or just do it with ansible and kubectl, *after* the bootstrap is done.

re: "function signatures are rarely--if ever--updated": Socorro's signature generation normalizes function names before using them for signature generation. I adjust that periodically to handle issues with functions that don't match across platforms (and probably other things, too). I know there are a few outstanding bugs with it now that I haven't gotten to, yet.

re: "crash signatures": I'm currently throwing together a web api. I might get it done by the end of next week--we'll see.

re: "address signatures": I don't think Socorro or Socorro users use address signatures. I don't think we generate or store them, but it's possible Socorro users can generate them from the data we do have. If they're interesting, I'm game for looking into adding them.

I think I mentioned this elsewhere but tossing it here too: currently "page is writeable/executable" is a fuzzy signal because it is considered best-practice for a JIT to use W^X for its generated code pages. Pages do in fact need to be updated, and they can need to be updated *while the code in that page is being semantically executed* (i.e. it's on the stack).

This means at the time of a crash, a totally valid return address could point to non-executable memory. We *really* want an explicit signal for "this page is a JIT page". I'm going to ask microsoft about the tools they have for this.
